_work.title: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
_work.creator: various
_work.place: Siberia, the Arctic, the Northwest Coast, Korea, the Himalaya
_work.language: Evenki / Buryat / Yakut / Nivkh / Nanai / Nganasan / Yupik / Tlingit / Korean / Nepali
_work.rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: A photographic and print survey of the ecstatic technique of the sacred across the circumpolar and Asian world: Siberian shaman costumes and painted drums (Evenki, Buryat, Yakut, Nganasan, Nivkh, Nanai, Tuvan), Inuit and Yupik shaman masks and the angakkuq, the Greenlandic tupilaq, Tlingit soul-catchers and Northwest-Coast spirit masks, Korean musindo shaman painting, Manchu shrines and Himalayan Kirati shamans — the drummers, soul-catchers and spirit-figures by which the shaman journeys between worlds.

entries.title: Evenki Shaman and Family (1907)
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: A. A. Makarenko (photographer)
entries.summary:
A 1907 ethnographic photograph of an Evenki (Tungus) shaman in full regalia, posed beside family members in the Yenisei taiga.

## Iconography
The shaman wears the characteristic **fringed coat** hung with **iron pendants**, straps and metal spirit-figures that ring and clash as the body moves, and a soft cap; the long leather fringes represent the feathers or fur by which the soul takes flight. The hanging iron discs are at once armour against hostile spirits and the dwelling-places of the shaman's helping spirits.

## Significance
Among the Tungus peoples — from whose language the very word *shaman* (Evenki *šamán*) entered European scholarship — the practitioner mediated between the human community and the spirit worlds, curing the sick by retrieving lost souls and escorting the dead. The weight of metal a costume carried was read as a measure of the shaman's accumulated power. Such field photographs underpin the comparative study of [[motif:shamanism|shamanism]] from Mircea Eliade onward.

## For artists & game designers
The costume is a wearable inventory: every pendant is a bound spirit, so a character's power can be shown literally by how much iron they clank with.

citations.text: Mircea Eliade, *Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy* (1951; Eng. 1964).

entries.title: Buryat Shaman
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Anonymous (National Library of Russia)
entries.repository: National Library of Russia, St Petersburg
entries.summary:
A historic photograph of a Buryat shaman of the Lake Baikal region in ceremonial dress.

## Iconography
The shaman stands robed and crowned, the costume hung with straps and metal; the antlered or horned headdress marks the wearer as one who travels in animal form. Buryat shamans carried *morin khuur* horse-staffs — the spirit-steeds on which the soul rides between the layered heavens of **Tengri**, the sky.

## Significance
Buryat shamanism, set against the Lake Baikal heartland, distinguished "white" celestial shamans from "black" shamans of the underworld and organised the cosmos into the fifty-five western and forty-four eastern *tengri*. The shaman ascended through these heavens in trance to plead for the community before the sky-gods. Soviet repression drove the practice underground in the twentieth century; its post-Soviet revival has made Buryatia a centre of living [[motif:shamanism|shamanism]].

## For artists & game designers
The horse-headed staff is the mount: a shaman who 'rides' a carved stick into the sky is a ready image for spirit-travel that needs no wings.

entries.title: Yakut (Sakha) Shaman Diorama
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Museum diorama (photographed by NComparato)
entries.summary:
A museum diorama reconstructing a Yakut (Sakha) *oyuun* shaman in the act of drumming, fully costumed.

## Iconography
The figure is shown mid-rite, the single-headed **frame drum** (*düngür*) raised in one hand and the beater in the other; the coat hangs heavy with fringes and iron. The drum is both vehicle and map — its skin a cosmogram, its beat the hoofbeat that carries the soul aloft.

## Significance
Among the Sakha of the Lena basin the *oyuun* (male) and *udagan* (female) shamans cured illness by combat with the spirits that caused it, drumming themselves into the trance in which the free-soul departs the body. The drum's rim, beater and pendants were each named and spirit-inhabited. Such reconstructions preserve, for the museum visitor, a practice that Christianisation and Soviet policy nearly extinguished.

## For artists & game designers
A dramatised tableau like this is a storyboard frame: the raised drum and frozen mid-step read instantly as 'entering trance.'

citations.text: Marjorie Mandelstam Balzer, *Shamans, Spirituality, and Cultural Revitalization* (2011).

entries.title: Shaman's Drum and Implements, Black Tartar (Altai) People
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Anonymous (Altai/Khakassia); photographed by Daderot
entries.summary:
An Altai ("Black Tartar") shaman's drum with its beater and hanging implements, collected in 1892 and held in Stockholm.

## Iconography
The oval **drum** is a wooden hoop stretched with hide; the membrane carries a painted **cosmogram** dividing the upper, middle and lower worlds, with sun, moon, riders and animal spirits. An iron cross-bar inside forms the grip, itself wound with pendants and ribbons; the beater is a padded stick. Each painted figure is a station on the route the shaman's soul will travel.

## Significance
The Altai *kam* mounted the drum as a steed and beat it to reach trance, ascending the painted world-tree to the sky-god Ülgen or descending to Erlik below. The drumhead is one of the most explicit surviving **maps of the shamanic cosmos**, the painted zones doubling as a divinatory field. Confiscation and burning of such drums by missionaries makes museum survivals like this precious documents.

## For artists & game designers
The drumhead is a self-contained cosmology diagram: copy its three-zone layout and you have an instant 'map of the spirit world' UI.

citations.text: Mircea Eliade, *Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy* (1964).

entries.title: Nganasan Shaman
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Ninara (photographer)
entries.summary:
A Nganasan shaman's costume of the Taimyr, the northernmost shamanic tradition of the Eurasian Arctic.

## Iconography
The heavy reindeer-hide coat is faced with long **fringes** and rows of metal, the headgear masking the face; the whole suit is conceived as the body of a spirit-bird or reindeer into which the shaman is transformed when worn. Coloured streamers stand for the rays of the journeying soul.

## Significance
The Nganasan, hunters of the Taimyr tundra, retained one of the last unbroken shamanic lineages into the twentieth century; the shaman Demnime and his successors were filmed and recorded by Soviet ethnographers, giving anthropology a rare living archive of the seven-day great rites. The costume's transformation of wearer into animal-spirit is the visual core of the [[motif:soul flight|soul-flight]] complex.

## For artists & game designers
A costume that turns its wearer into a reindeer-spirit is a transformation item: equip the coat, become the animal.

citations.text: Jean-Luc Lambert & Roberte Hamayon, studies of Nganasan ritual.

entries.title: Nivkh Shaman (Sakhalin)
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Uncredited photographer (V. M. Doroshevich, *Sakhalin*)
entries.summary:
A Nivkh (Gilyak) shaman of Sakhalin Island, reproduced in V. M. Doroshevich's exposé of the penal colony *Sakhalin* (Part II).

## Iconography
The shaman is shown drumming amid onlookers, the **frame drum** held high; the loose belted coat and headband are hung with the metal and cloth tokens of the helping spirits. The scene captures the communal nature of the rite — the assembled community is part of the performance.

## Significance
The Nivkh of the lower Amur and Sakhalin practised a riverine and maritime shamanism, the shaman (*ch'am*) curing and divining for hunters and fishers and mediating with the master-spirits of bear, sea and river. The Nivkh bear-festival was among the great ritual complexes recorded by Lev Shternberg, who studied them while himself exiled to Sakhalin. Doroshevich's reformist travelogue inadvertently preserved this ethnographic image.

## For artists & game designers
The surrounding crowd matters: shamanic healing is public theatre, not a solitary spell — stage it with an audience.

citations.text: Lev Shternberg, *The Social Organization of the Gilyak* (posth. 1933).

entries.title: Belt of a Nanai Shaman
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Anonymous (Nanai); photographed by Lapot
entries.place: Amur River region, Russian Far East
entries.repository: Center of Traditional Folk Culture of the Middle Urals
entries.summary:
A Nanai (Goldi) shaman's ceremonial belt, a band hung with rows of iron cones and pendants.

## Iconography
The leather belt (*yampa*) carries dozens of forged-iron **cones and discs** that hang from straps; struck into motion by the shaman's dancing, they produce the metallic roar that drives off hostile spirits and marks the rhythm of the journey. Each cone is a small bell-tongued voice of a helping spirit.

## Significance
The Nanai of the Amur dressed the shaman in coat, apron and this sounding belt; the mass of iron, like the Tungus costume, both armoured the wearer and housed the *seven* and other spirit-helpers. Nanai shamanism was documented in detail by Soviet ethnographers and through the celebrated shamaness lineages of the twentieth century. The belt is a portable instrument: the body becomes a percussion section in trance.

## For artists & game designers
A belt that rattles louder the harder you dance is a tidy mechanic — sound as a weapon against the unseen.

citations.text: Anna-Leena Siikala, *The Rite Technique of the Siberian Shaman* (1978).

entries.title: Tuvan Shamans
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Irgit (photographer)
entries.summary:
Tuvan shamans (*kham*) of southern Siberia in feathered regalia with their frame drums.

## Iconography
The shamans wear the **feathered headdress** that veils the eyes and the long-fringed coat hung with cloth-bound *eeren* spirit-figures and ribbons; each holds the single-headed **drum** that serves as steed and shield. The feathers signal the bird into which the soul transforms for its flight.

## Significance
Tuvan shamanism, intertwined with Tibetan Buddhism in this Inner-Asian crossroads, survived Soviet suppression to become a vigorous post-1990 revival, with registered shamanic societies in Kyzyl. The *kham* heals, divines and conducts funerary rites, journeying on the drum-steed through the layered sky. Western interest, from Eliade to recent ethnomusicology of Tuvan throat-singing, has made Tuva emblematic of living Siberian [[motif:shamanism|shamanism]].

## For artists & game designers
The eye-veiling feather fringe is a great costume cue: a shaman who 'sees' only with closed, hidden eyes reads as inwardly journeying.

entries.title: Manchu Shamanic Shrine, Beijing
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Anonymous (19th-century engraving)
entries.summary:
A nineteenth-century engraving of a Manchu shamanic shrine in Beijing, showing the apparatus of Qing court and household shamanism.

## Iconography
The interior shows the **spirit-pole** (*šomo*) and altar with offerings; Manchu *saman* worship combined the worship of Heaven, the ancestral spirits and the household gods. The standing pole, topped to receive the sky-spirit, anchors the open-air portion of the rite.

## Significance
The Manchu carried a clan-based shamanism into the imperial palace, where the **Tangse** shrine and the Kunning Palace housed regular shamanic sacrifices throughout the Qing dynasty — a rare case of state-sponsored shamanism. The *Code of Shamanic Sacrifices* (1747) standardised these rites in print. The tradition links the court to the wider Tungus-Manchu shamanic world of the Amur and Siberia.

## For artists & game designers
State shamanism is an underused setting: imagine a palace with an official spirit-medium on the payroll and a published rulebook of rites.

citations.text: Nicola Di Cosmo, studies of Qing shamanic ritual.

entries.title: Mansindo (Painting of the Myriad Spirits)
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Anonymous (Korean)
entries.summary:
*Mansindo* (萬神圖, "Painting of the Myriad Spirits"), an early-nineteenth-century Korean shamanic shrine painting depicting the pantheon a *mudang* serves.

## Iconography
The painting arrays rows of the **spirit-generals**, mountain-gods, royal ancestors and tutelary deities (*musin*) in court and military dress, each frontally enthroned in the hieratic register of Korean *musindo*. These portraits hung in the shaman's shrine and were the seats into which the spirits descended during the *gut* ritual.

## Significance
The Korean **mudang** — overwhelmingly female — is possessed by the *musin* in the *gut*, a sung-and-danced ceremony of healing, divination and propitiation. The *musindo* paintings make the otherwise invisible pantheon present and addressable; the spirit-general (*janggun*) figures embody martial power against malign forces. Joseon Confucian elites scorned the practice, yet it persisted as the dominant religion of the village and household.

## For artists & game designers
A wall of enthroned spirit-portraits that the medium 'wears' one at a time is a clean possession mechanic — each painting a selectable character.

citations.text: Laurel Kendall, *Shamans, Housewives, and Other Restless Spirits* (1985).

entries.title: Kirati Shamans (Jhakri), Nepal
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Rai504089 (photographer)
entries.summary:
Kirati *jhakri* shamans of the eastern Nepalese hills with their double-headed drums.

## Iconography
The shamans wear the white robe and crossed-bandolier of cowrie and bead, with porcupine-quill or feather headgear, and beat the round **two-headed drum** (*dhyangro*) with a curved stick; the drum's frame-handle is often carved with a spirit-face. The white dress and rhythmic drumming mark the *jhakri*'s ascent into trance.

## Significance
The **jhakri** is the shaman of many Himalayan peoples — Kirati (Rai, Limbu), Tamang, Magar and others — diagnosing affliction as the work of witches, ghosts or offended deities and curing it by drumming, soul-retrieval and the symbolic climb of the cosmic tree or mountain. The *dhyangro* drum and the *mundhum* oral scripture of the Kirat anchor a living South-Asian shamanism distinct from both Hindu and Buddhist orthodoxy.

## For artists & game designers
The double-headed drum with a carved spirit-face on its handle is a fine prop — an instrument that watches as it plays.

citations.text: John T. Hitchcock & Rex L. Jones, eds., *Spirit Possession in the Nepal Himalayas* (1976).

entries.title: Yup'ik Mask with Seal or Sea-Otter Spirit
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Anonymous (Yup'ik); photographed by Daderot
entries.summary:
A nineteenth-century Yup'ik dance mask from the Yukon River, carved to embody the spirit (*yua*) of a seal or sea-otter.

## Iconography
The painted wooden mask sets a distorted human face within concentric hoops and appended limbs, feathers and gut-cord; the small animal face is the **inua/yua**, the indwelling person of the seal, shown peering through its surrounding spirit-world. The asymmetry and attached paws render the moment of transformation between human and animal being.

## Significance
Yup'ik masks were carved to a shaman's (*angalkuq*) vision and danced in the winter *Agayuyaraq* ceremonies to honour and summon the **yua** of the game animals, ensuring their return to the hunters. Most were destroyed or discarded after a single use, so survivors are rare; their visionary geometry profoundly influenced the Surrealists, who collected them. The mask is a [[motif:soul flight|soul-flight]] made carvable.

## For artists & game designers
A mask built as concentric rings around a tiny animal face literally frames 'the spirit inside the spirit' — nested souls you can open like rooms.

citations.text: Ann Fienup-Riordan, *The Living Tradition of Yup'ik Masks* (1996).

entries.title: Ikpukhuak and His Shaman Wife Higalik
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: George Hubert Wilkins (photographer)
entries.summary:
A photograph by Hubert Wilkins of the Copper Inuit man Ikpukhuak and his wife Higalik (Ice House), a practising *angakkuq*, made during the Canadian Arctic Expedition.

## Iconography
The couple stand in caribou-fur parkas; the photograph documents not regalia but the ordinary appearance of an Arctic shaman — for among the Inuit the **angakkuq** wore no special costume, the power residing in helping spirits (*tuurngait*) and sewn amulets rather than in dress.

## Significance
The **angakkuq** healed the sick, controlled weather and hunting luck, and undertook the perilous soul-journey to the sea-woman **Sedna** at the ocean floor to comb the sins from her hair and release the game. Knud Rasmussen's Fifth Thule Expedition recorded these beliefs across the Arctic. That a shaman might be an ordinary-looking married woman like Higalik underlines how the office, not the outfit, made the angakkuq.

## For artists & game designers
No costume, all power: a shaman indistinguishable from anyone else until the spirits arrive is a strong subversion of the feathered-stereotype.

citations.text: Knud Rasmussen, *Intellectual Culture of the Copper Eskimos* (1932).

entries.title: Inuit Medicine Man and Boy (1900)
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Anonymous (photographer)
entries.summary:
A photograph of an Inuit "medicine man" (*angakkuq*) with a boy, dated about 1900.

## Iconography
The shaman stands in heavy fur dress, the young companion at his side suggesting the apprentice relationship by which shamanic knowledge passed. As in the Copper-Inuit photographs, the power is invisible — carried in spirit-helpers and amulets rather than displayed on the body.

## Significance
The **angakkuq** was made, not born: a candidate underwent initiatory hardship and instruction by an elder shaman, sometimes culminating in a vision of his own skeleton (the "reduction to the bones" Eliade saw as a near-universal shamanic motif). Photographs like this, taken by missionaries and traders at the turn of the century, are among the few visual records of Arctic shamanic practice before its suppression by the churches.

## For artists & game designers
The master-and-apprentice pairing is the whole arc in one frame: the boy is the player you will become.

citations.text: Daniel Merkur, *Becoming Half Hidden: Shamanism and Initiation among the Inuit* (1985).

entries.title: Tupilak (Kârale Andreassen)
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Kârale Andreassen (Greenlandic artist)
entries.summary:
A depiction of a *tupilak*, the Greenlandic Inuit avenging spirit-monster, by the East Greenlandic artist Kârale Andreassen.

## Iconography
The **tupilak** is shown as a composite monstrous being assembled from animal and human parts — exactly as it was originally made: a witch or aggrieved person built the creature in secret from bones, skin, hair and a piece of the intended victim, animating it with chants to hunt the enemy across the sea-ice.

## Significance
The **tupilak** belongs to the dark, aggressive pole of Inuit spirit-craft: a curse given a body. Its danger was double-edged, for if the victim possessed stronger magic the tupilak would turn and destroy its maker. Because the original objects were perishable and hidden, almost none survive; it is from carved-ivory souvenirs and from drawings like Andreassen's — he was the son of the angakkuq Mitsivarniánga — that the form is known. The tupilak is the Arctic's signature [[motif:shamanism|shamanic]] weapon.

## For artists & game designers
A homemade monster sewn from spare parts that turns on its summoner if they're too weak is a complete summoning-with-consequences rule.

entries.title: Tlingit Soul-Catcher (Bentwood)
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Anonymous (Tlingit); photographed by Kabuto 7
entries.summary:
A Northwest-Coast **soul-catcher** (*ixt'* charm), a small double-mouthed tube of bone or horn carved in Tlingit formline.

## Iconography
The hollow charm is open at both ends, each terminating in an animal head — typically a two-headed sea-monster or bear — and inlaid with abalone; the central band carries a small face. Worn on a cord at the shaman's neck, the tube was the vessel into which a captured wandering soul could be stoppered.

## Significance
The Tlingit and Tsimshian **shaman** (*ixt'*) diagnosed sickness as the theft or straying of the patient's soul; with the soul-catcher he trapped the errant soul and blew it back into the body, sealing the tube with shredded cedar. The double-headed form expresses the charm's two open mouths, ever ready to seize a passing soul. It is one of the most explicit ritual instruments of [[motif:shamanism|soul-retrieval]] anywhere.

## For artists & game designers
A literal container for souls, worn around the neck, with a mouth at each end — pure inventory-magic, ready to be a key item.

citations.text: Aldona Jonaitis, *Art of the Northern Tlingit* (1986).

entries.title: Shamans' Masks, Tlingit (c. 1830)
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Anonymous (Tlingit); photographed by Daderot
entries.repository: Nelson-Atkins Museum of Art, Kansas City
entries.summary:
A set of Tlingit shamans' masks of about 1830, each carved as the face of a particular helping spirit.

## Iconography
The wooden masks, painted and inlaid in Northwest-Coast **formline**, show human and animal-spirit faces; a Tlingit *ixt'* owned a series of such masks, one for each of his **yek** (spirit-helpers), donning them in turn to manifest each spirit during a curing or divining performance.

## Significance
The Tlingit **shaman** inherited his office and his spirits within the clan, his power signalled by long uncut hair and a kit of masks, rattles, amulets and the soul-catcher. In the masked dance the *ixt'* became, successively, each of his *yek*, the audience watching the spirits arrive one by one. Such masks — collected heavily in the late nineteenth century — are among the supreme achievements of Northwest-Coast carving and a vivid record of [[motif:shamanism|spirit-embodiment]].

## For artists & game designers
A rack of masks where each face = one summonable spirit is a clean ability-loadout: swap the mask, swap the power.

citations.text: Aldona Jonaitis, *Art of the Northern Tlingit* (1986).

entries.title: Mask of Nulmal, Kwakwaka'wakw Winter Ceremonial
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Anonymous (Kwakwaka'wakw); from Boas, via Internet Archive
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: illustration in F. Boas, *The Social Organization... of the Kwakiutl* (1897)]
entries.summary:
The mask of **Nulmal**, the "Fool Dancer" spirit of the Kwakwaka'wakw (Kwakiutl) winter ceremonial, illustrated in Franz Boas's monograph.

## Iconography
The mask shows a grotesque face with an exaggerated, mucus-streaming nose — the mark of Nulmal, the wild fool who guards the cannibal-spirit dancer and punishes any breach of ritual order. The plate is one of Boas's engraved figures of the *tseka* (winter) dance regalia.

## Significance
In the **Tseka** or winter ceremonial the Kwakwaka'wakw enacted the abduction and taming of initiates by powerful spirits, above all the cannibal **Baxwbakwalanuksiwe'** served by the *hamatsa* dancer; Nulmal and the other attendant spirits policed the rite. Boas's fieldwork with George Hunt made this the most fully documented Northwest-Coast ceremonial complex and a foundation of American anthropology. The dances dramatise spirit-possession and return — the shamanic journey turned into theatre.

## For artists & game designers
A 'fool' spirit whose job is to punish players who break ritual rules is a built-in enforcement NPC for any ceremony minigame.

citations.text: Franz Boas, *The Social Organization and the Secret Societies of the Kwakiutl Indians* (1897).

entries.title: Religious Costumes (Larousse) — Shaman among World Clergy
entries.work: Shamanism & Spirit-Magic of the Circumpolar & Asian World
entries.creator: Étienne Mahler (engraver), Larousse du XXᵉ siècle
entries.summary:
A comparative plate of religious costume from the *Larousse du XXᵉ siècle* (1932), ranging the world's ritual dress — including Asian shamanic and priestly figures — across a single encyclopedia page.

## Iconography
The engraved sheet sets rows of robed figures side by side: Christian clergy and monastic orders, ancient and Jewish priests, and Asian ritual specialists, each captioned, in the synoptic display typical of the great French encyclopedias. The shamanic and "exotic" figures appear as entries in a taxonomy of sacred dress.

## Significance
The plate is a document of **reception** rather than of shamanism itself: it shows how early-twentieth-century European reference works absorbed the world's spirit-specialists into a comparative grammar of religion, flattening Siberian and Asian shamanic costume into one cell of a costume chart. This encyclopedic ordering — heir to Bernard Picart's eighteenth-century *Cérémonies et coutumes religieuses* — shaped how the lay European public pictured "the shaman."

## For artists & game designers
A single comparative costume chart is a ready reference sheet: line up your world's clergy side by side and the visual language of each order reads at a glance.

citations.text: Bernard Picart, *Cérémonies et coutumes religieuses de tous les peuples du monde* (1723–37), the genre's ancestor.
